annotations.label: Binary search tree

anchor.snippet: is a rooted binary tree data structure with the key of each internal node being greater than all the keys in the respective node's left subtree and less than the ones in its right subtree

annotations.note: Ordnode is a weight-balanced BST whose left<node<right ordering invariant is captured by Ordnode.Bounded, but a plain (unbalanced) BST is not separately defined.

annotations.label: Time complexity linear in tree height

anchor.snippet: The time complexity of operations on the binary search tree is linear with respect to the height of the tree.

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Tree.height but proves no running-time/complexity bounds for tree operations.

annotations.label: Logarithmic lookup performance

anchor.snippet: each comparison skips about half of the remaining tree

annotations.note: No complexity/halving analysis of search is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Average and worst-case complexity

anchor.snippet: The complexity analysis of BST shows that, on average

annotations.note: Average- and worst-case complexity analysis is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Binary search tree (strict total order)

anchor.snippet: A binary search tree is a rooted binary tree in which nodes are arranged in strict total order

annotations.note: Ordnode.Bounded enforces that every element lies strictly between its lo/hi bounds, encoding the BST strict-order arrangement.

annotations.label: Worst-case degeneracy to linked list

anchor.snippet: successive operations in the binary search tree may lead to degeneracy and form a singly linked list

annotations.note: Mathlib's Ordnode is always kept balanced, and no degeneration-to-list result is stated.

annotations.label: Search procedure

anchor.snippet: Searching begins by examining the root node

annotations.note: Ordnode.find implements the recursive compare-at-root-then-descend BST search, though the prose procedure itself is not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: BST search running time

anchor.snippet: the running time complexity of BST search is

annotations.note: The running-time bound of BST search is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Successor of a node

anchor.snippet: is the node with the smallest key greater than

annotations.note: Ordnode.findGt returns the smallest element of the tree greater than a given value, matching the successor's defining property (no correctness lemma proven).

annotations.label: Predecessor of a node

anchor.snippet: is the node with the largest key smaller than

annotations.note: Ordnode.findLt returns the largest element of the tree less than a given value, matching the predecessor's defining property (no correctness lemma proven).

annotations.label: New nodes inserted as leaves

anchor.snippet: New nodes are inserted as leaf nodes in the BST

annotations.note: Ordnode.insert (with insert.valid preserving the invariant) implements insertion, but it rebalances rather than always leaving the new element as a leaf.

annotations.label: Deletion case: leaf node

anchor.snippet: is a leaf node, it is replaced by

annotations.note: Ordnode.erase implements BST deletion, but the specific leaf-case behavior is not isolated as a theorem.

annotations.label: Deletion case: one child

anchor.snippet: gets elevated by modifying the parent node

annotations.note: Ordnode.erase handles single-child deletion within its rebalancing recursion, but the one-child case is not stated separately.

annotations.label: Deletion case: two children

anchor.snippet: has both left and right children

annotations.note: When erasing a two-child node, Ordnode.erase calls Ordnode.glue to merge the two subtrees, the analogue of the successor-replacement case.

annotations.label: Inorder tree walk

anchor.snippet: followed by the root node and right subtree

annotations.note: Ordnode.toList performs an in-order traversal (toList_node = toList l ++ x :: toList r, i.e. left, root, right).

annotations.label: Inorder walk yields non-decreasing order

anchor.snippet: visits all the nodes in the order of non-decreasing key sequence

annotations.note: toList and the Bounded ordering invariant exist, but no lemma proving that toList of a valid Ordnode is sorted/non-decreasing is present.

annotations.label: Preorder tree walk

anchor.snippet: The root node gets visited first, followed by left and right subtrees

annotations.note: Tree.traverse (and Tree.map) visit in node → left → right order, i.e. preorder, though no list-producing preorder function is named as such.

annotations.label: Postorder tree walk

anchor.snippet: followed by the right subtree, and finally, the root

annotations.note: No post-order (left, right, root) traversal is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Degeneration without rebalancing

anchor.snippet: insertions or deletions in a binary search tree may lead to degeneration

annotations.note: Mathlib only models the self-balancing Ordnode; degeneration of unbalanced BSTs is not formalized.

annotations.label: Height-balanced tree

anchor.snippet: A tree is height-balanced if the heights of the left sub-tree and right sub-tree are guaranteed to be related by a constant factor

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Tree.height but has no height-balance (AVL-style) predicate; its Ordnode.Balanced is a size/weight-balance condition instead.

annotations.label: Weight-balanced tree

anchor.snippet: the criterion of a balanced tree is the number of leaves of the subtrees

annotations.note: Ordnode.Balanced/BalancedSz is a weight-balance criterion on subtree sizes (Adams/Nievergelt–Reingold bounded-balance trees), a specific instance of the weight-balanced-tree notion.

annotations.label: Tree sort

anchor.snippet: where all the elements are inserted at once and the tree is traversed at an in-order fashion

annotations.note: Ordnode.ofList (foldr insert) followed by Ordnode.toList realizes tree sort, but no theorem proves the composite output is sorted.

annotations.label: Ascending priority queue removal

anchor.snippet: removal of an element with the lowest priority is done through leftward traversal of the BST

annotations.note: Ordnode.eraseMin (with findMin) removes the leftmost/minimum element as in min-priority extraction, but the priority-queue framing is not formalized.

annotations.label: Descending priority queue removal

anchor.snippet: removal of an element with the highest priority is done through rightward traversal of the BST

annotations.note: Ordnode.eraseMax (with findMax) removes the rightmost/maximum element as in max-priority extraction, but the priority-queue framing is not formalized.
